stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{2} + [[b]]x \). By the Mean Value Theorem, there is a point \( c \) in \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[q]])-f([[p]])}{[[q]]-[[p]]} \). Find this value of \( c \).
solution: Here \( f'(x)=2x+[[b]] \) and the average rate of change is \( \dfrac{f([[q]])-f([[p]])}{[[q]]-[[p]]} = [[p]]+[[q]]+[[b]] \). Setting \( 2c+[[b]] = [[p]]+[[q]]+[[b]] \) gives \( c = \dfrac{[[p]]+[[q]]}{2} = [[answer]] \), the midpoint of the interval (as always for a quadratic).
distractor_rationales: This is the left endpoint of the interval, not the guaranteed interior point.
This is the right endpoint of the interval, not the guaranteed interior point.
This forgets to divide the sum of the endpoints by 2.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x} \). By the Mean Value Theorem applied on \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \), there is a point \( c \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[q]])-f([[p]])}{[[q]]-[[p]]} \). Which of the following is this value of \( c \)?
solution: Here \( f'(x)=-\dfrac{1}{x^{2}} \), and the average rate of change simplifies to \( -\dfrac{1}{[[p]][[q]]} \). Setting \( -\dfrac{1}{c^{2}} = -\dfrac{1}{[[p]][[q]]} \) gives \( c^{2}=[[p]][[q]] \); since \( c \) must lie in \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \) (both negative here), \( c = -\sqrt{[[p]][[q]]} = [[answer]] \), the negative geometric mean of the endpoints.
distractor_rationales: This is the left endpoint of the interval, not the guaranteed interior point.
This is the right endpoint of the interval, not the guaranteed interior point.
This is the geometric mean with the wrong sign; it lies OUTSIDE the interval \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \) since both endpoints are negative.
This uses the arithmetic mean of the endpoints instead of the geometric mean that this function's Mean Value Theorem calculation actually requires.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[amp]]\sin([[k]]x) \), so \( f(0) = f\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{[[k]]}\right) = 0 \). By Rolle's theorem, which of the following is a value \( c \) in \( \left(0, \dfrac{\pi}{[[k]]}\right) \) satisfying \( f'(c)=0 \)?
solution: Here \( f'(x)=[[amp]][[k]]\cos([[k]]x) \), which vanishes when \( [[k]]x = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + n\pi \). The only such point inside \( \left(0,\dfrac{\pi}{[[k]]}\right) \) is \( c = \dfrac{\pi}{[[twok]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the right ENDPOINT of the interval, where \( f \) is zero, not where \( f' \) is zero.
This is too small: \( \cos([[k]]x) \) has not yet reached zero at this point.
This is the NEXT zero of \( \cos([[k]]x) \) after the given one, which lies outside the interval \( \left(0,\pi/[[k]]\right) \).
This doubles the correct value, placing it outside the interval.
This is off by one from the correct value.
